original_reasoning: استرغزه = found him weak, softened him (استضعفه واستلانه). στραγγίζω = to squeeze out, strain. The phonetic similarity is striking (r-gh-z vs str-ng-z). Semantically: making something soft/yielding relates to squeezing — both involve applying pressure to extract compliance or liquid. Pressing to soften and pressing to squeeze share a force-on-substance core.
cloudiness can stand opposite to clarity, giving a plausible negation-based bridge to 'clear'.
Greek 'to the nail' carries a concrete edge or exact-end sense; Arabic gloss centers on roots, bases, and firm embeddedness, giving a plausible structural semantic chain.
Arabic udm = condiment/seasoning that flavors food, mixed with bread. Greek Demeter = goddess of grain and food. Both are central to the food/eating concept; udm flavors what Demeter provides.

السكركة = شراب الذرة (a drink made from sorghum/millet — a thick fermented grain beverage). γλίσχρος = sticky, viscous, adhesive, claggy. A fermented grain drink is characteristically thick and sticky in texture. The Arabic names the substance, the Greek names its primary physical quality. This is a genuine material-property match.

Arabic shatama = insult/curse; mashtum = insulted one. Greek stomati = dat.sg of stoma 'mouth'. Insulting is done with the mouth — direct action-instrument link. Strong concrete link, with notable phonetic resemblance shtm/stom.
Arabic catching up or reaching and Greek passing through both sit in the field of directed movement across a course.

الإثم = sin, being burdened with guilt (آثم، أثيم، مأثوم). λυόμεθα = we are being released/loosened. This is a genuine conceptual pair: the concept of being freed from sin (λύω τὰς ἁμαρτίας) is central to both Semitic and Greek religious thought. Release from guilt is the natural complement of guilt itself.
